slides.title: Module 3 — Allergens Part 1: Know the Risk
slides.subtitle: SQF Food Safety Training
slides.notes: Welcome to Module 3. This is the start of the heart of our food safety program — allergen control. In Part 1 we build the awareness: what a food allergen is, the nine that matter most, which of them are in the products we make, and why one of those products carries a claim that makes our plant different from any ordinary bakery. Get this foundation right and Part 2, where we cover how we protect against allergens, will make complete sense.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.lead: By the end of this module you will be able to:

items: Explain what a food allergen is and why even a trace can be dangerous
Name the top 9 allergens regulators require us to control
Map which allergens are in each of our product lines
Describe the free-from claim that makes our plant different

slides.notes: Here is what you will walk away with. Four things: what a food allergen actually is and why even a trace amount can hurt someone, the nine allergens that regulators require every food plant to control, which of those allergens live in each of the product lines we make, and the free-from claim on one of those products that makes our plant different from a normal bakery. Part 1 is about knowing the risk; Part 2 will cover how we control it.

slides.title: What is a food allergen?
slides.lead: A protein the body mistakes for a threat — and overreacts.

steps: A normal food protein enters the body
An allergic person's immune system treats it as an attacker
A reaction starts in minutes to hours

slides.notes: A food allergen is just a protein found in a normal food — like the protein in milk, egg, or wheat. For most people it's harmless. But in someone with an allergy, the immune system wrongly treats that protein as a dangerous attacker and overreacts. A reaction can begin within minutes to a few hours, and at its worst it becomes anaphylaxis — a sudden, whole-body reaction that can close the airway and kill. That is why we take allergens so seriously.

slides.title: Allergy is not the same as intolerance
slides.lead: We control for the one that can kill.

items: Triggered by a tiny trace amount
Reaction can be sudden and severe
Can be fatal — this is what we protect against
Usually needs a larger amount
Uncomfortable, but not life-threatening
Not the hazard our controls target

visual.chip: Treat every allergen as if a life depends on it

slides.notes: People sometimes mix up allergy and intolerance, but they are very different. A food allergy is an immune reaction: a tiny trace can set it off, it can come on fast and severe, and it can be fatal. That is the hazard our entire allergen program is built to protect against. An intolerance, like trouble digesting lactose, usually takes a larger amount, is uncomfortable rather than dangerous, and is not what our controls target. When we say allergen, we mean the serious one — treat every allergen as if a life depends on it, because it can.

slides.title: The Top 9 Allergens
slides.lead: These nine cause the large majority of serious reactions.

left.header: The four already in our plant
items: Wheat
Egg
Milk
Soy
Peanuts
Tree nuts
Fish
Shellfish
Sesame — the 9th, added in 2023

slides.notes: Regulators name nine major allergens that cause the large majority of serious reactions, and every food plant must control them. Four of them already live in our plant: wheat, egg, milk, and soy. The other five to recognize are peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, and sesame — and note that sesame was added as the ninth required allergen in 2023, so it's newer and easy to overlook. Learn all nine by name: milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy, and sesame.

slides.title: Allergens in the products we make
slides.lead: Know what's running on the line before you touch it.

items: The rum cakes we make — contain wheat, egg, and milk
The biscotti we make — contain wheat and egg, and may contain tree nuts
The allergen-free product we make — labeled free of all top 9 allergens

slides.notes: Here is the allergen map of what we actually make. The rum cakes we make contain wheat, egg, and milk. The biscotti we make contain wheat and egg and may contain tree nuts. And the allergen-free product we make is labeled free of all top nine allergens. Before you work a line, you need to know which of these is running, because the same building makes both allergen-containing products and a product whose whole selling point is that it has none.

slides.title: The claim that makes us different
slides.lead: Labeled free of all 9 — made beside them.
slides.photo: PHOTO: allergen-free product on the production line

body: Labeled free of all top 9 allergens — the promise on the package.
Yet made in the same building, on shared lines and rotating staff.
One trace makes the claim false — and can seriously hurt someone.
visual.chip: Protecting the claim is everyone's job

slides.notes: This is what sets our plant apart from an ordinary bakery. The allergen-free product we make is labeled free of all top nine allergens — that is the promise printed on the package and the reason customers trust it. But here's the challenge: it's made in the same building that runs wheat, egg, milk, and tree-nut products, on shared equipment and rotating staff. If even a trace of those allergens reaches the product, the claim becomes false and a consumer who trusted that label can be seriously hurt. Protecting that claim is not one person's job — it's everyone's.

slides.title: What an allergen mistake costs
slides.lead: It doesn't stop at one package.

steps: An allergen gets into a product that shouldn't have it
A label no longer tells the truth
Recall, lost trust, and a failed audit follow

slides.notes: An allergen mistake never stops at one package. It starts when an allergen gets into a product that isn't supposed to contain it. Now the label is wrong — it no longer tells the truth about what's inside. A customer who relies on that label can have a reaction, possibly a severe one. And from there it cascades: a product recall, lost trust from our customers, and a failed audit. Undeclared allergens are one of the most common reasons food gets recalled. One slip on the floor can undo everything.

slides.title: What cross-contact looks like
slides.lead: Allergens move in ways you can't always see.

items: Shared equipment or utensils not fully cleaned between products
Airborne flour or powder settling onto a free-from line
Hands, gloves, or sleeves carrying residue from another area
Reworking or mixing leftovers into the wrong batch

slides.notes: Cross-contact is how an allergen ends up somewhere it shouldn't, and it often happens invisibly. It can come from shared equipment or utensils that weren't fully cleaned between products. It can be airborne — flour or powder drifting and settling onto a free-from line. It can ride in on hands, gloves, or sleeves carrying residue from another area. And it can come from rework — mixing leftover product into the wrong batch. You don't need a big spill; a trace is enough. Part 2 covers exactly how we prevent each of these — for now, learn to spot them.

slides.title: Your role in knowing the risk

items: Know which products and allergens run on your line each shift
Read the label and never assume a product is allergen-free
Treat the free-from product line as the highest-care job in the plant
Report any spill, mix-up, or uncertainty before product moves

slides.notes: Before we ever get to procedures, awareness itself is a control. Know which products and allergens are running on your line each shift. Read the label and never just assume a product is allergen-free — confirm it. Treat the free-from product line as the highest-care job in the building, because it is. And if you see a spill, a mix-up, or you're simply not sure, stop and report it before the product moves. Speaking up early is always cheaper than a recall.

slides.title: Summary

items: An allergen is a protein that can trigger a severe, even fatal reaction from a trace
Know the top 9 — four of them already run in our plant
The allergen-free product we make is labeled free of all 9, made beside allergen products
Knowing the risk and reporting it is everyone's first job
visual.next: Next: Allergens Part 2 — Protecting the Claim

slides.notes: Let's recap Part 1. An allergen is an ordinary food protein that, in an allergic person, can trigger a sudden and even fatal reaction from just a trace. There are nine major allergens to know, and four of them — wheat, egg, milk, and soy — already run in our plant. The allergen-free product we make is labeled free of all nine even though it's made right beside allergen-containing products, which is why protecting that claim is everyone's job. Knowing the risk and reporting anything that looks wrong is your first responsibility. Up next, Part 2: how we actually protect the claim on the floor. Great work.

quiz.question: What is a food allergen?
options: A germ that causes food poisoning and illness in people
Any ingredient that simply has a very strong taste
A chemical cleaning agent used to sanitize equipment
A protein the immune system wrongly treats as a threat
quiz.hint: It's about the immune system overreacting to a normal food.
quiz.rationale: An allergen is a normal food protein that an allergic person's immune system mistakes for a threat, triggering a reaction that can be severe.

quiz.question: How much of an allergen can trigger a serious allergic reaction?
options: Even a tiny trace amount
Only a large serving
About half a product
It never happens from food contact
quiz.hint: Think about why we worry about invisible residue.
quiz.rationale: Even a trace amount can trigger a reaction — which is why cross-contact and residue matter so much.

quiz.question: Which best describes the difference between an allergy and an intolerance?
options: They are essentially the same thing in the body
An intolerance is actually far more dangerous than a true allergy
An allergy is an immune reaction that can be fatal; intolerance isn't
Only food intolerances are regulated by law, not allergies
quiz.hint: One involves the immune system and can be life-threatening.
quiz.rationale: An allergy is an immune reaction that can be sudden and fatal from a trace; an intolerance is a digestive issue, uncomfortable but not life-threatening. Our controls target allergies.

quiz.question: How many major allergens do regulators require food plants to control?
quiz.hint: Sesame became the most recent addition in 2023.
quiz.rationale: There are 9 major allergens: milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy, and sesame (added 2023).

quiz.question: Which allergens are already present in the products we make?
options: None at all — our plant is completely allergen-free
Only peanuts are present anywhere in the building
Only sesame is handled anywhere in the plant
Wheat, egg, and milk, plus tree nuts in some
quiz.hint: Think about the rum cakes and biscotti.
quiz.rationale: The rum cakes we make contain wheat, egg, and milk; the biscotti we make contain wheat and egg and may contain tree nuts. Soy is also present in the plant.

quiz.question: What makes the allergen-free product we make different and high-risk?
options: It is labeled free of the 9 allergens yet made beside them
It is by far the most expensive product we make
It actually contains every single one of the allergens
It is made in a separate dedicated facility with no other products
quiz.hint: The risk is the claim plus the shared building.
quiz.rationale: The product is labeled free of all top 9 allergens yet is produced in the same building, on shared equipment and rotating staff, as wheat/egg/milk/tree-nut products — so any cross-contact makes the claim false.

quiz.question: Which of these is an example of allergen cross-contact?
options: Properly wearing your hairnet correctly out on the production floor
Carefully reading the product label before use
Airborne flour settling on a free-from line, or shared utensils
Washing your hands thoroughly before taking a break
quiz.hint: It's how an allergen reaches a product that shouldn't have it.
quiz.rationale: Cross-contact includes airborne powder, un-cleaned shared equipment/utensils, residue on hands or gloves, and mixing rework into the wrong batch.

quiz.question: You're not sure whether the line was fully cleaned before the free-from product run. What should you do?
options: Start the run; it's probably fine
Stop and report it before product moves
Wipe it quickly and say nothing
Switch products to use it up
quiz.hint: Awareness and speaking up early is the first control.
quiz.rationale: If you're unsure, stop and report before product moves. Speaking up early is always cheaper and safer than a recall caused by undeclared allergens.
